heb.paragraph: "Show me your ways (dərāk̅ek̅ā), LORD, teach me your paths" (v.4). An alphabetic acrostic prayer. David asks for guidance, forgiveness, and instruction. Key themes: trust (v.2), teaching (vv.4–5), mercy (v.6), forgiveness (v.7), humility (v.9).

refs.note: "Trust in the LORD with all your heart... he will make your paths straight" — the wisdom parallel.
Moses' prayer 'teach me your ways so I may know you' — the same request the psalmist makes in 25:4, connecting personal piety with the Mosaic covenant tradition.
David's penitential appeal to God's compassion and steadfast love — the same cluster of covenant vocabulary that grounds Psalm 25's plea for forgiveness.

notes.note: "Show me your ways, teach me your paths, guide me in your truth." Three requests: show, teach, guide. David wants direction, not just information.
"Remember your mercy and love, for they are from of old. Do not remember the sins of my youth." Mercy remembered; sins forgotten. David asks God for selective memory.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the acrostic structure (each verse begins with a successive Hebrew letter) imposes formal constraint on a deeply personal prayer. Art and anguish coexist — the poet disciplines grief into pattern.
Alter: 'ways…paths…truth' — three synonyms for divine instruction, yet each adds nuance. Derek is the road; 'ōraḥ is the beaten track; 'emet is reliability. The psalmist wants direction he can trust.
Calvin: the prayer for guidance presupposes human inability to find the way alone. Calvin: we do not merely need a map — we need a guide, because sin has blinded us to the path that is already there.

Calvin: 'Do not remember the sins of my youth' — Calvin finds here the universal human experience: looking back with shame on earlier folly. The appeal is not to merit but to God's own goodness (ṭûv).

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'I lift up my soul' — the idiom (nāśā' nefeš 'el) means to direct one's desire or trust toward something. The soul is not 'raised' spatially but oriented relationally toward God.
NET: 'For the sake of your name' — the appeal to God's name (šēm) is an appeal to his reputation and character. Forgiveness serves God's own glory, not merely the sinner's relief.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: 'teach me' appears three times in vv.4-5. This is not academic curiosity but existential urgency — a lost traveller asking for the road, not a student browsing the curriculum.
Kidner: 'loving and faithful' — ḥesed and 'emet paired together, as they so often are. God's love is not sentimental (it is faithful); God's faithfulness is not cold (it is loving).

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: 'your great mercy and love' — raḥamîm (tender compassion) and ḥesed (covenant love) form the double foundation of the psalmist's appeal. Both are rooted in God's character, not human worthiness.

VanGemeren: 'Good and upright is the Lord; therefore he instructs sinners' — God's moral perfection is not a barrier to sinners but the very reason he teaches them. Redemptive-historically, this anticipates the gospel.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the psalm opens with trust vocabulary — 'trust' (bāṭaḥ), 'hope' (qāwâ), 'shame' (bôš). The fundamental anxiety is social: will trusting God lead to public humiliation?
Goldingay: the covenant (berît) is mentioned for the first time at the psalm's centre. The relationship between guidance and covenant is causal: God guides because he has bound himself to his people.

hist.context: The first acrostic psalm. Each verse begins with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet. David asks God to lead, teach, guide, and forgive. The mood is humble and teachable.

sections.header: Verses 12–22 — “The LORD Confides in Those Who Fear Him”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD confides (sōd) in those who fear him" (v.14). Sōd is intimate counsel — the inner circle’s conversation. God shares his secrets with the reverent. Fear of the LORD produces not distance but intimacy.

refs.note: "I no longer call you servants... I have called you friends" — the NT fulfilment of God’s intimate counsel.
"He takes the upright into his confidence" — the same sōd theology.

notes.note: "The LORD confides in those who fear him." The paradox: fear produces intimacy. Reverence opens the door to friendship.
"My eyes are ever on the LORD." Sustained attention. David’s gaze is fixed. The direction of the eyes determines the direction of the life.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'The Lord confides' — sôd means intimate counsel, a private circle of trust. The image is startling: the cosmic Creator shares secrets with those who fear him.
Alter: the acrostic breaks down slightly here as the poet's composure gives way. The personal pronouns multiply — 'I am lonely,' 'my troubles,' 'my anguish,' 'my sins.' Form yields to feeling.
Calvin: God's 'secret counsel' is not esoteric knowledge but the experience of covenant intimacy. Those who fear God understand his purposes — not because they are clever but because he draws near.
Calvin: the psalm ends by widening from 'me' to 'Israel.' Calvin: private prayer rightly concludes with public concern. The individual does not hoard God's attention but intercedes for the community.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'confides' — sôd can mean 'council' (a group), 'counsel' (advice), or 'secret' (confidential information). Here the sense is intimate friendship — God takes the reverent into his confidence.

NET: this verse falls outside the acrostic pattern (the Hebrew letter pe follows the expected sequence ending at taw in v.21). It may be a liturgical addition expanding the personal psalm to communal use.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: 'my eyes are ever on the Lord' — the remedy for entrapment is not looking at the snare but looking at the deliverer. Kidner: fixation on the problem feeds despair; fixation on God feeds faith.
Kidner: loneliness and affliction come together. Isolation intensifies suffering; community would ease it. The psalmist's first need is not a solution but a companion — 'Turn to me.'

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the covenant-intimacy motif connects to the prophetic tradition — God does nothing without revealing his plan to his servants (Amos 3:7). Fear of the Lord opens access to divine purpose.
VanGemeren: the communal ending transforms private lament into corporate intercession. The individual's troubles mirror Israel's troubles; personal redemption anticipates national deliverance.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: three benefits of fearing Yahweh: instruction in the right way (v.12), prosperity and inheritance (v.13), and intimate friendship with God (v.14). The rewards move from external to relational.
Goldingay: 'how fiercely they hate me' — the Hebrew is śin'at ḥāmās, 'hatred of violence.' These enemies do not merely dislike the psalmist; they wish him physical harm. The threat is concrete.

hist.context: God guides the humble (v.9), confides in the God-fearing (v.14), frees from snares (v.15). David’s eyes are always on the LORD (v.15). The psalm closes with a plea for Israel’s redemption (v.22).

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David asks God to lead, teach, guide, and forgive…

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: David asks God to lead, teach, guide, and forgive…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVDo not remember the sins of my youth and my rebellious ways; according to your love remember me, for you, Lord, are good.ESVRemember not the sins of my youth or my transgressions; according to your steadfast love remember me, for the sake of your goodness, O LORD!NIVRemember, Lord, your great mercy and love, for they are from of old.ESVRemember your mercy, O LORD, and your steadfast love, for they have been from of old.

rec.text: ""Show me your ways, teach me your paths, guide me in your truth." Three requests: show, teach, guide. David wants direction, not just information."

rows.label: The Intimacy — 25:12–22
rows.text: "The LORD confides in those who fear him." Intimacy through reverence. Eyes always on God.

hebtext.note: "Show me your ways (dərāk̅ek̅ā), LORD, teach me your paths" (v.4). An alphabetic acrostic prayer. David asks for guidance, forgiveness, and instruction. Key themes: trust (v.2), teaching (vv.4–5), mercy (v.6), forgiveness (v.7), humility (v.9).

"The LORD confides (sōd) in those who fear him" (v.14). Sōd is intimate counsel — the inner circle’s conversation. God shares his secrets with the reverent. Fear of the LORD produces not distance but intimacy.

thread.text: "Trust in the LORD with all your heart... he will make your paths straight" — the wisdom parallel.
"I no longer call you servants... I have called you friends" — the NT fulfilment of God’s intimate counsel.
"He takes the upright into his confidence" — the same sōd theology.

themes.note: Mercy: the plea for forgiveness and guidance. Faith: eyes always on the LORD. Covenant: God’s intimate counsel with the faithful.
